title: Exponential Time Differencing Schemes for a Phase-Field Model of Multicomponent Membranes
authors: Wangbo Luo, Zhonghua Qiao, Yanxiang Zhao
abstract: In this paper, we develop and analyze exponential time differencing (ETD) schemes for a phase-field model of multicomponent membranes proposed in our previous work \cite{luo2025ohta}, in which membrane deformation is governed by a force-balance phase-field equation and protein segregation is described by a membrane-associated Ohta-Kawasaki (OK) dynamics. For a fixed phase-field membrane, we introduce a geometry-adapted operator splitting method based on the localization function, which reformulates the surface OK dynamics into a form suitable for ETD integration. The resulting first- and second-order ETD schemes, combined with finite-difference spatial discretization, are rigorously proved to satisfy a discrete maximum-bound principle and unconditional energy stability. For the coupled system, we construct stabilized ETD schemes in an FFT-based spectral framework, treating stiff linear terms exactly and nonlinear mechanochemical couplings explicitly. A narrow-band implementation further reduces the computational cost by restricting surface calculations to the diffuse membrane region. Numerical experiments confirm the predicted temporal accuracy, maximum-bound preservation, and energy decay for the fixed-membrane OK problem, and demonstrate stable and efficient three-dimensional simulations of protein-driven pattern formation and membrane deformation.
summary: This paper introduces and analyzes Exponential Time Differencing (ETD) schemes to simulate the complex dynamics of multicomponent membranes. The authors first prove the stability of these schemes for the simplified case and then apply them to the full, coupled system governing membrane mechanics and protein segregation. This provides a robust and efficient numerical tool for studying pattern formation in biological membranes.
main_problem: Developing stable, accurate, and efficient numerical schemes to simulate the complex, coupled dynamics of multicomponent membranes.
main_result: The authors rigorously prove that the developed Exponential Time Differencing (ETD) schemes satisfy the discrete maximum-bound principle and unconditional energy stability for the fixed-membrane case.
method: The core method involves applying ETD schemes (ETD1 and ETDRK2) combined with operator splitting and spectral collocation to solve the coupled phase-field and Ohta-Kawasaki dynamics.
model_or_system: The system models multicomponent membranes using a force-balance phase-field equation coupled with membrane-associated Ohta-Kawasaki (OK) dynamics for protein segregation.
key_observables: Discrete energy decay, preservation of the discrete maximum-bound principle (MBP), protein-driven pattern formation, and membrane deformation.
important_parameters: Repulsion strength ($\gamma$), biochemical strength ($\alpha$), and numerical parameters like time step ($	au$) and spatial resolution ($h$).
main_assumptions: The initial rigorous analysis assumes a fixed phase-field membrane geometry; extending to the fully coupled, evolving system requires careful stabilization and splitting.
figures_summary: Numerical experiments confirm predicted temporal accuracy and energy decay for the fixed-membrane OK problem, and demonstrate stable 3D simulations of protein-driven pattern formation.
paper_structure: The work first establishes the ETD schemes and proves stability for the simplified, fixed-geometry OK problem. It then extends this framework to the full, coupled system, employing spectral methods and narrow-band techniques for efficiency.
why_interesting: While the physics is condensed matter/soft matter, the focus on developing and proving the stability of advanced time integration schemes (ETD) for stiff, coupled partial differential equations is highly relevant to computational physics.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
